message:
feat(cli): add `pi info <extension>` command (bd-2uh1.3)

Show detailed information about an extension from the discovery index:
- Name, ID, description (word-wrapped)
- Tags, license, source (npm/git/url)
- Install command hint
- Fuzzy fallback: if exact name/id not found, shows top search hit

Also adds Info variant to conformance fixture runner's command_value().

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1uy.1.5","title":"Hostcall ABI: Consolidate Duplicate HostCallPayload/HostResultPayload Types","description":"# Goal
Eliminate (or strictly contain) duplicated hostcall ABI type definitions so the ABI cannot drift:
- `crate::extensions::{HostCallPayload, HostResultPayload, HostStreamChunk, ...}`
- `crate::connectors::{HostCallPayload, HostResultPayload, HostStreamChunk, ...}`

# Why This Is Not Cosmetic
Right now, the two type graphs are **not identical** (real drift already exists):
- `extensions::HostStreamChunk` includes `backpressure` fields; `connectors::HostStreamChunk` does not.
- error helpers in `connectors` produce `output: null` on errors, while protocol validation requires `output` is an object.

If we keep two competing definitions, we will eventually ship a protocol/schema mismatch.

# Scope
1. Choose a single canonical home for the ABI structs/enums.
   - Recommended: make `extensions::*` canonical, since it is tied directly to `ExtensionMessage` protocol/schema.

2. Make the non-canonical module:
   - re-export the canonical types, OR
   - become a thin newtype wrapper with explicit conversions.

3. Fix helper constructors to be spec-compliant everywhere:
   - `host_result_err*` must set `output: {}` (object), not `null`.
   - Ensure `error` presence matches `is_error`.

4. Update `HttpConnector` and any other connector code to use canonical types.

# Tests
- Compile-time:
  - ensure only one canonical type is used across modules (no parallel structs).
- Runtime:
  - round-trip serialize/deserialize between connector boundary and protocol boundary.
  - assert that any `HostResultPayload` produced by helpers passes `validate_host_result()`.
- Drift sentinel:
  - a unit test that asserts the JSON Schema (`docs/schema/extension_protocol.json`) matches the Rust type serialization shape (including backpressure fields).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] There is exactly one source-of-truth for hostcall ABI structs/enums.
- [ ] All helper constructors return schema-valid payloads.
- [ ] All call sites compile and tests pass.
- [ ] A drift sentinel test would fail if future fields are added to one side but not the other.","notes":"2026-02-06 HazyLynx: Consolidated connectors hostcall ABI types to canonical extensions types via re-export in src/connectors/mod.rs; added canonical-type compile sentinel + schema drift sentinel tests; validations: cargo test --lib connectors::tests (7 passed), cargo check --all-targets passed, cargo test --lib sse::tests passed.  {"id":"bd-1uy.2","title":"Auth: Refresh expired OAuth tokens for extension providers (startup + deterministic logs)","description":"# Goal
Ensure extension-registered OAuth providers behave correctly in real runs by wiring automatic refresh for **expired extension OAuth tokens**.

# Background (Observed)
`src/auth.rs` implements `AuthStorage::refresh_expired_extension_oauth_tokens(...)` with unit tests, but there is no call site in the main runtime.

Current behavior risk:
- extension provider tokens can silently expire and never refresh unless the user re-auths
- conformance/E2E that rely on extension providers can become flaky or fail unexpectedly

# Scope
1) **Define refresh policy (spec-level)
- When to refresh:
  - on startup before running extensions
  - optionally just-in-time before first use of an extension provider
- What to refresh:
  - only expired tokens
  - skip built-in providers handled by `refresh_expired_oauth_tokens_with_client` (e.g. anthropic)
- Failure handling:
  - network failure: keep old token, emit actionable error/log event
  - invalid refresh token: mark token invalid and require re-login

2) **Wire call sites**
- Ensure extension configs (oauth provider configs) are available at the time refresh runs.
- Call refresh in all relevant entrypoints:
  - interactive mode
  - non-interactive (print)
  - RPC mode
- Ensure refresh respects:
  - timeouts
  - redaction policy (never log secrets)
  - deterministic structured logs for harness diffing

3) **Logging / Artifacts**
- Emit `pi.auth.refresh` events to the unified logging sink with:
  - provider id
  - token state transition (redacted)
  - timing
  - outcome classification
- Ensure bd-4u9 artifact index + redaction summary are used by any E2E script.

# Tests
- Unit tests: already exist for the refresh function; add any missing edge cases.
- Integration test:
  - execute a minimal runtime path that triggers the refresh call and asserts auth.json mutation
  - use VCR playback (no live network)
- E2E scenario:
  - drive a run where an extension provider token is expired and is refreshed automatically
  - capture deterministic JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9

## Acceptance Criteria
- Expired extension OAuth tokens refresh automatically without manual user intervention.
- Refresh behavior is deterministic under test harness control.
- No secrets leak into logs/artifacts.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Refresh policy is explicitly defined (startup vs JIT) and implemented
[ ] Refresh is wired for all modes (interactive, print, RPC)
[ ] No secrets leak into logs; redaction summary present for E2E artifacts
[ ] Integration/E2E coverage proves the refresh call site executes (VCR playback; no live network)
[ ] JSONL logs + artifacts per bd-4u9 where applicable
[ ] Quality gates pass: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T08:02:21.387414725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T10:02:53.786405649Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T10:02:53.786318427Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["auth","extensions","oauth","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1uy.2","depends_on_id":"bd-1uy","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T08:02:21.387414725Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":535,"issue_id":"bd-1uy.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Completed: Wired extension OAuth token refresh into main startup path.

Changes:
1. **src/main.rs** (~lines 369-401): After extension model entries are computed, builds a HashMap<String, OAuthConfig> from entries with oauth_config, then calls auth.refresh_expired_extension_oauth_tokens() with graceful error handling (warns on failure, continues startup).

2. **src/auth.rs** (~lines 282-314): Added structured tracing to refresh_expired_extension_oauth_tokens():
   - pi.auth.extension_oauth_refresh.start (info, with count)
   - pi.auth.extension_oauth_refresh.ok (info, per-provider, with elapsed_ms)
   - pi.auth.extension_oauth_refresh.error (warn, per-provider, with elapsed_ms)

3. **tests/extensions_provider_oauth.rs**: Added 5 new integration tests:
   - oauth_configs_from_entries_empty_when_no_oauth
   - oauth_configs_from_entries_extracts_providers_with_oauth
   - full_wiring_refresh_expired_token_via_mock_server (uses mock HTTP server)
   - full_wiring_no_refresh_when_token_valid
   - full_wiring_refresh_skips_providers_without_config

All 38 tests pass (20 existing + 5 new + 13 common). Lib compiles clean.","created_at":"2026-02-06T10:02:47Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1v10","title":"Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup","description":"# Phase 0: Environment and Runtime Setup

## Purpose
Before we can do ANY differential testing, we need BOTH runtimes working and verified independently. This phase ensures:
1. The pi-mono TypeScript runtime can load and run extensions via Bun
2. The Rust QuickJS runtime can load and run extensions
3. A deterministic test environment exists (no randomness, fixed timestamps)

## Why This Is Phase 0
Everything else depends on this. If either runtime cannot load extensions, differential testing is impossible. We discovered Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun. The pi-mono source lives at legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/. We need to verify it builds and its extension loader works.

## Acceptance Criteria
- pi-mono extension loader.ts can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Rust QuickJS runtime can load the hello.ts extension and produce output
- Both outputs can be captured as structured JSON
- Deterministic mode eliminates timestamps, random values, cwd-dependent paths","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.671337047Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918993495Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T17:36:15.918905561Z","close_reason":"Phase 0 complete: pi-mono loads extensions via Bun/jiti (tests/ext_conformance/ts_oracle/load_extension.ts), Rust QuickJS runtime loads extensions (tests/ext_conformance_diff.rs), both produce structured JSON, all 60 official extensions validated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1v10","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:14:07.724213001Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":309,"issue_id":"bd-1v10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"KEY DISCOVERY: Bun 1.3.8 is available at /home/ubuntu/.bun/bin/bun, which also provides a node shim. pi-mono uses jiti for extension loading (not native Bun module resolution). The virtualModules mechanism in loader.ts maps package names to bundled versions (@sinclair/typebox, @mariozechner/pi-agent-core, etc.). In development mode it uses aliases instead. For our harness, we need to run in development mode (not Bun binary mode) so that jiti resolves imports via filesystem.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:27:14Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1ve4","title":"CI: add cross-platform build/test matrix (linux/macOS/windows)","description":"# Goal
Increase release confidence by running CI on all target platforms (at minimum build + unit tests), and document any platform-specific exclusions.

# Background
The current CI workflow only runs on `ubuntu-latest`. That’s good for fast iteration but insufficient for shipping binaries.

# Scope
- Add a GitHub Actions matrix over:
  - `ubuntu-latest`
  - `macos-latest`
  - `windows-latest`
- For each OS:
  - `cargo fmt --check`
  - `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
  - `cargo test --all-targets`

# Platform Considerations
- System deps:
  - `fd` and `rg` are required/optional for tool tests; install equivalents per OS.
- Path dependencies:
  - CI currently clones sibling repos (asupersync/rich_rust/charmed_rust/sqlmodel_rust). Ensure the same approach works on macOS/Windows.
- VCR playback mode:
  - Keep `VCR_MODE=playback` for determinism.

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ci.yml` runs on Linux/macOS/Windows.
- [ ] Any OS-specific skips are explicit and justified (no silent test omissions).
- [ ] Failures include enough logs to debug platform-specific issues.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

 {"id":"bd-2uh1","title":"Epic: Extension Discovery and Search — user-facing browsing, search, and extension info","description":"# Goal
Build the user-facing discovery layer so users can find, evaluate, and install extensions without needing to know exact package names or GitHub URLs ahead of time.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already handles install/remove/update from npm, git, and local sources. The extension catalog (bd-3kp3) covers the internal manifest for pinning and corpus tracking. But there is NO user-facing discovery mechanism:
- Users cannot search for extensions by keyword
- Users cannot browse categories
- Users cannot see extension details before installing
- There is no quality signal (popularity, compat score, maintenance status)

This is the difference between "apt install package-name" (exists) and "apt search keyword" + "apt show package-name" (doesnt).

# Why This Matters
- Extension ecosystems live or die by discoverability — VSCode Marketplace, npm search, crates.io
- Without discovery, adoption is bottlenecked by word-of-mouth and documentation
- The conformance corpus already has 60+ validated extensions — users should be able to find them
- This is table stakes for any extension platform

# Scope
1. Registry protocol: how extension metadata is published and queried
2. CLI commands: pi search, pi info, pi list --available
3. Rich terminal rendering: extension READMEs, install counts, compat scores
4. Curated collections: categories and featured lists
5. Quality signals: conformance tier, last-updated, Pi version compatibility

# Out of Scope
- Hosting a central registry server (use GitHub API + npm registry as backends for now)
- Extension submission/review process (manual curation initially)
- Monetization or paid extensions

# Architecture
- Data source: GitHub API for repo metadata + npm registry for package metadata
- Local index: JSON cache of discovered extensions, refreshed periodically
- Search: Client-side fuzzy search over cached index (no server needed)
- Rendering: rich_rust tables for search results, glamour markdown for README rendering
- Categories: tags in extension manifest (pi.json or package.json pi field)

# Dependencies
- Depends on bd-3kp3 (Extension catalog, pinning, corpus manifest) for manifest schema
- Relates to bd-ofcj (Expand proven Pi extension coverage) for corpus to search over
- Relates to bd-34io (Classify & score candidates) for quality signals

# Children
1. Extension registry protocol spec + index schema
2. pi search CLI command
3. pi info CLI command (rich detail view)
4. Curated collections and category taxonomy
5. Quality signals computation and display","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:35:20.108245219Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:09.018450686Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","extensions","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","depends_on_id":"bd-34io","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:25.343192162Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","depends_on_id":"bd-3kp3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:24.967841239Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":502,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ARCHITECTURAL CONTEXT: The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) handles install/remove/update. This epic builds the DISCOVERY layer on top. Think of it as: package_manager = pip install, this epic = PyPI search + package detail pages.

DESIGN DECISION — NO CENTRAL SERVER: We deliberately avoid hosting a registry server. Instead, we query GitHub API and npm registry as data sources, and cache the results locally. This keeps the system decentralized and avoids the operational burden of running infrastructure. The curated manifest is a static JSON file in the Pi repo.

SEARCH IMPLEMENTATION: Client-side fuzzy search over the cached index. This is fast enough for 1000+ extensions and avoids server round-trips. If the corpus grows beyond 10K, we can add SQLite full-text search (the dependency already exists via asupersync).

QUALITY SIGNALS ARE CRITICAL: The difference between "show me extensions" and "show me GOOD extensions" is the quality signal layer. Without it, users install broken/abandoned extensions and have a bad experience. The conformance tier (from our test suite) is the most valuable signal because it's the only one we control.

DEPENDENCY: bd-3kp3 (Extension catalog, pinning, corpus manifest) must define the manifest schema first. This epic consumes that schema. bd-34io (Classify & score candidates) provides the quality signals we display.

ESTIMATED EFFORT: Registry protocol (6-8h), pi search (8-10h), pi info (6-8h), collections (4-6h), quality signals (6-8h). Total: ~30-40 engineering hours.","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:02:41Z"},{"id":515,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC-LEVEL):
- [ ] All 6 subtasks (registry, search, info, collections, quality, tests) closed
- [ ] pi search <query> returns relevant results from cached index
- [ ] pi info <name> shows extension details with rendered README
- [ ] Quality signals visible in search results (conformance tier, maintenance status)
- [ ] Offline mode: search works on cached index when no network

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.1","title":"Extension registry protocol spec and local index schema","description":"# Goal
Design the extension registry protocol — how extension metadata is fetched from remote sources (GitHub, npm), stored locally, and queried for search/browse operations.

# Background
The package manager (src/package_manager.rs) already knows how to install from npm and GitHub. But there is no structured index of AVAILABLE extensions. Users must know exact package names. This task designs the metadata layer that enables discovery.

# Deliverables

1. Remote data sources:
   - GitHub: Search GitHub repos with topic "pi-extension" via GitHub API
   - npm: Search npm registry for packages with "pi-extension" keyword
   - Curated list: static JSON manifest in a Pi-maintained repo (for quality-vetted extensions)

2. Local index schema (SQLite or JSON):
   {
     "version": 1,
     "last_refreshed": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
     "extensions": [
       {
         "id": "auto-commit-on-exit",
         "name": "Auto Commit on Exit",
         "description": "Automatically commits changes when Pi session ends",
         "version": "1.2.0",
         "source": { "type": "npm", "package": "@user/pi-auto-commit" },
         "author": "username",
         "license": "MIT",
         "tags": ["git", "automation", "lifecycle"],
         "conformance_tier": 1,
         "pi_version_range": ">=0.5.0",
         "downloads_30d": 1500,
         "last_updated": "2026-01-10T00:00:00Z",
         "readme_url": "https://...",
         "repository_url": "https://..."
       }
     ]
   }

3. Refresh strategy:
   - On first "pi search": fetch and cache index
   - Auto-refresh if cache older than 24 hours
   - Manual refresh: "pi update-index"
   - Incremental: only fetch new/updated since last refresh

4. Search algorithm:
   - Fuzzy text match on name + description + tags
   - Weighted scoring: name match (3x) > tag match (2x) > description match (1x)
   - Sort by relevance * quality_signal (conformance tier, downloads)

# Files to Create/Modify
- New: src/extension_index.rs (index management, search, refresh)
- src/package_manager.rs: Wire index into install flow
- src/config.rs: Add index cache path

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Schema documented in docs/extension-registry.md
- [ ] Index refresh from GitHub + npm sources works
- [ ] Fuzzy search returns relevant results
- [ ] Cache invalidation after 24h
- [ ] Index stored in ~/.pi/agent/extension-index.json","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonMill","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T19:28:22.543081221Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T19:28:22.543058038Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discovery","extensions","protocol"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:57:37.363576504Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":520,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"DESIGN REFINEMENT (from review):

The registry spec MUST be offline-first:
1. Ship a bundled seed index with the Pi binary (embedded at compile time)
2. First search uses seed index if no network (no "please wait" on first run)
3. Background refresh when network becomes available
4. Stale cache warning ("Index is 7 days old, run pi update-index to refresh")
5. Never fail on network error — always fall back to cached/seed index

This matters because:
- New users running pi search for the first time shouldnt wait for a network fetch
- Users on restricted networks (corp firewalls, planes) should still discover extensions
- The seed index can be updated with each Pi release (60+ known extensions)","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:58:41Z"},{"id":560,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Progress update (CrimsonMill): wired extension-index runtime integration and shorthand source resolution.

-{"id":"bd-2uh1.2","title":"pi search CLI command — search available extensions by keyword","description":"# Goal
Implement the "pi search <query>" CLI command that searches the extension index and displays results in a rich terminal table.

# Background
This is the primary user-facing discovery command. It queries the local extension index (built by the registry protocol spec task) and presents results in a scannable format.

# Usage
  pi search git          # Search for git-related extensions
  pi search "auto commit" # Phrase search
  pi search --tag provider # Filter by tag
  pi search --sort downloads # Sort by popularity
  pi search --limit 20     # Limit results

# Output Format (rich_rust table)
  Name              Version  Description                        Downloads  Tier
  auto-commit       1.2.0    Auto commit on session exit         1,500     T1
  git-branch-hook   0.5.0    Switch branches via /git command      890     T2
  git-stats         2.0.1    Show git statistics in session        650     T1

  3 extensions found. Install with: pi install <name>

# Implementation Plan
1. Add "search" subcommand to clap CLI (src/cli.rs)
2. In main.rs, route to search handler
3. Search handler:
   a. Load or refresh extension index
   b. Apply fuzzy search with query
   c. Apply filters (--tag, --tier, --source)
   d. Sort results (--sort: relevance (default), downloads, name, updated)
   e. Render rich_rust table with columns: Name, Version, Description, Downloads, Tier
   f. Print install hint at bottom

4. Auto-refresh:
   - If index doesnt exist or is >24h old, refresh before searching
   - Show "Refreshing extension index..." message during refresh
   - Cache refreshed index for subsequent searches

- [ ] Results sorted by relevance by default

- [ ] --sort flag works (relevance, downloads, name, updated)

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.4","title":"Curated extension collections and category taxonomy","description":"# Goal
Define the category taxonomy for extensions and create curated collections (featured, popular by category, starter packs) that help users discover relevant extensions.

# Background
Raw search only works when users know what to look for. Categories and collections enable browsing — "show me all provider extensions" or "what are the most popular automation extensions?" This is how every extension marketplace (VSCode, Chrome Web Store, Obsidian) drives discovery beyond search.

# Deliverables

1. Category taxonomy:
   - providers: Custom LLM providers (OpenAI, local models, etc.)
   - tools: Additional tools beyond the built-in 7
   - automation: Lifecycle hooks, auto-commit, scheduled tasks
   - git: Git-related extensions (branch management, commit helpers)
   - formatting: Output formatting, rendering, themes
   - data: Data processing, analysis, transformation
   - testing: Test runners, coverage reporters
   - security: Audit, scanning, policy enforcement

2. Curated collections:
   - "Getting Started": 5 essential extensions for new users
   - "Power User": 10 extensions for advanced workflows
   - "Provider Pack": All custom provider extensions
   - "DevOps Toolkit": CI/CD, deployment, monitoring extensions

3. Manifest format for collections:
   {
     "id": "getting-started",
     "name": "Getting Started",
     "description": "Essential extensions for new Pi users",
     "extensions": ["auto-commit-on-exit", "git-branch-hook", ...]
   }

4. CLI commands:
   - pi search --category providers   # Browse by category
   - pi collections                    # List available collections
   - pi collections getting-started   # Show collection details

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add category and collection support
- src/cli.rs: Add collections subcommand
- New: data/collections.json (curated collection definitions)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Category taxonomy covers all known extension types in corpus
- [ ] At least 3 curated collections defined
- [ ] pi search --category works
- [ ] pi collections command shows available collections
- [ ] Categories derived from extension manifest tags","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:17.011431603Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T06:47:16.109286971Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T06:47:15.946199840Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["curation","discovery","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T06:58:17.011431603Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2uh1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T07:01:24.337971802Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":715,"issue_id":"bd-2uh1.4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closed.  {"id":"bd-2uh1.5","title":"Quality signals computation and display for extension discovery","description":"# Goal
Compute and display quality signals for extensions in search results and info views, helping users assess extension reliability before installing.

# Background
Not all extensions are equal. Some are well-tested (conformance tier 1), actively maintained, and widely used. Others are experimental, abandoned, or incompatible. Quality signals help users make informed decisions — like star ratings on app stores or crate download counts on crates.io.

# Quality Signals

1. Conformance Tier (from Pi conformance test suite):
   - T1: Fully tested, all assertions pass
   - T2: Mostly tested, minor issues
   - T3: Partially tested, some features fail
   - Untested: No conformance data

2. Maintenance Status:
   - Active: Updated within 90 days
   - Maintained: Updated within 1 year
   - Stale: Not updated in 1+ year
   - Abandoned: Not updated in 2+ years, no response to issues

3. Compatibility Score (from compat scanner):
   - Green: 100% compatible, no Node.js dependencies
   - Yellow: 90%+ compatible, minor shim gaps
   - Red: <90% compatible, significant issues

4. Popularity Metrics:
   - npm downloads (30d) if from npm
   - GitHub stars if from GitHub
   - Install count from Pi telemetry (future)

5. Composite Quality Score (0-100):
   - 40% conformance tier
   - 25% maintenance status
   - 20% compatibility score
   - 15% popularity metrics

# Display Format
In search results table: colored badge (green/yellow/red) + numeric score
In info view: detailed breakdown of each signal

# Implementation Plan
1. Compute signals at index refresh time
2. Store in extension index entry
3. Sort by composite score in search results (default)
4. Display badge in search table column
5. Detailed breakdown in pi info output

# Files to Modify
- Extension index module: Add quality computation
- Search/info display code: Add quality columns/sections

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 signal types computed for indexed extensions
- [ ] Composite score calculated with documented weights

 {"id":"bd-2uh1.6","title":"Tests: Extension Discovery E2E suite — search, info, index refresh, offline mode","description":"# Goal
End-to-end test suite for the extension discovery system, validating search, info display, index management, and edge cases like offline mode and stale caches.

# Test Categories

## 1. Index Management Tests (8+ tests)
- First-run: index created from scratch (mock GitHub + npm responses via VCR)
- Index refresh: stale index (>24h) triggers auto-refresh
- Incremental update: only new/modified extensions fetched
- Cache corruption: graceful recovery from malformed index file
- Offline mode: search works on cached index when network unavailable
- Manual refresh: pi update-index forces full refresh
- Index versioning: migration from v1 to v2 schema
- Concurrent access: two pi instances dont corrupt index

## 2. Search Tests (10+ tests)
- Exact name match: pi search "auto-commit" → returns exact match first
- Fuzzy match: pi search "git comit" → returns git commit extensions
- Tag filter: pi search --tag provider → only provider extensions
- Category filter: pi search --category automation
- Sort by downloads: pi search --sort downloads → descending order
- Sort by name: pi search --sort name → alphabetical
- Limit: pi search --limit 5 → exactly 5 results
- Empty results: pi search "xyznonexistent" → helpful message
- Special characters: pi search "c++" → handles special chars
- Long query: pi search "an extension that helps with git management" → reasonable results

## 3. Info Display Tests (6+ tests)
- Basic info: pi info auto-commit → shows metadata panel
- README rendering: README markdown rendered as terminal output
- Missing README: graceful fallback message
- Capabilities section: shows required permissions
- Install hint: correct install command shown
- Not found: pi info nonexistent-extension → helpful error

## 4. Quality Signals Tests (5+ tests)
- Conformance tier displayed correctly (T1/T2/T3)
- Maintenance status computed from last-updated date
- Composite score calculated with correct weights
- Badge colors match risk level (green/yellow/red)
- Score updates on index refresh

## 5. CLI Integration Tests (5+ tests)
- pi search output is valid table (parseable columns)
- pi info output fits terminal width
- pi collections lists available collections
- pi search from within interactive TUI (/search command)
- Error messages include help text

# Logging Requirements
Every test MUST emit structured JSONL logs:
{
  "test": "search_exact_match",
  "timestamp": "2026-...",
  "query": "auto-commit",
  "filters": {},
  "result_count": 3,
  "top_result": { "name": "auto-commit-on-exit", "score": 0.95 },
  "pass": true,
  "duration_ms": 45,
  "index_state": "cached",
  "artifacts": ["search-output.txt"]
}

# Test Infrastructure
- VCR cassettes for GitHub API and npm registry responses
- Mock index files for offline tests
- tempdir for index cache isolation
- Terminal width simulation for output tests

- [ ] VCR cassettes for all network calls

 {"id":"bd-39q8","title":"Phase 2: Custom streamSimple handler for extension providers","description":"Add support for custom streaming handlers in extension-registered providers.

## Background
Phase 1 (bd-1oj9) implements basic provider registration with standard APIs.
This phase adds support for custom streaming via JavaScript callbacks.

## API Addition
```typescript
pi.registerProvider(name, {
  // ... base config
  streamSimple?: (request) => AsyncIterable<string>
});
```

## Implementation Requirements
1. Async callback bridge from Rust to JS
2. Stream chunks from JS handler to agent loop
3. Error handling for JS exceptions during streaming
4. Cancellation support (cancel JS stream on interrupt)

## Technical Challenges
- JS -> Rust async iteration
- Back-pressure handling
- Error propagation across language boundary

## Test Plan
- Unit test: custom streamSimple called for provider
- Integration test: streaming works end-to-end
- Test: cancellation stops JS stream

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions.rs (stream bridge)
- src/extensions_js.rs (async callback support)
- src/provider.rs (use extension stream)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:36:36.961318176Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T05:30:01.456330680Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T05:29:25.285844625Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-39q8","depends_on_id":"bd-1oj9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-05T01:36:59.163581435Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":247,"issue_id":"bd-39q8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closed.  {"id":"bd-3am2","title":"Workstream: Error UX (actionable suggestions + context-aware hints)","description":"# Goal
Improve error UX so failures are not just "what happened" but also "what to do next" (actionable suggestions) and "why this happened here" (context-aware hints).

# Background
Pi runs in many environments (different terminals, missing system deps like `fd`, missing API keys, VCR playback mode, etc.). The port already has structured error types, but the user experience depends on:
- concise, readable rendering
- correct categorization
- a small set of high-signal remediation hints

# Principles
- Hints must be **actionable** (specific command/path/setting), not generic.
- Hints must be **non-spammy**: show at most a couple of suggestions per error.
- Hints must be **deterministic** and testable (unit/integration tests assert on messages).

# Candidate Hint Categories (examples)
- Missing API key → point to `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / etc and settings override.
- Missing system dependency (`fd`, `rg`) → exact install commands per OS.
- VCR cassette missing in CI → explain `VCR_MODE` + cassette dir + how to record.
- Permission denied reading/writing files → show the path and suggest chmod/ownership checks.
- Invalid config JSON → show file path + first parse error location.
- Provider auth failure (401) → suggest `/login` or `--api-key` usage.

# Deliverables
- A mapping from core error variants → short "next steps" hints.
- Rendering integration for:
  - interactive TUI error panels
  - print mode stderr
  - RPC mode error payloads (where appropriate)
- Tests validating the most important hint paths.

# Non-Goals
- Not building a full diagnostics engine.
- Not adding web browsing or auto-fixing.","acceptance_criteria":"[ ] Scope in description implemented fully with no feature loss

 {"id":"bd-3nix","title":"Workstream: /reload Parity (resources + autocomplete + diagnostics)","description":"# Goal
Bring `/reload` behavior to legacy parity.

# Legacy Behavior
In pi-mono (`interactive-mode.ts`), `/reload`:
- reloads the session resources
- applies themes
- rebuilds autocomplete
- (if extensions enabled) sets up extension shortcuts
- rebuilds chat from messages
- shows loaded resources summary
- surfaces models.json errors

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `/reload` calls `ResourceLoader::load(...)` and reports counts.
- It does not:
  - rebuild autocomplete
  - show models.json parse errors
  - reload context files (AGENTS)
  - reload extensions (beyond current extension workstream)

# Scope / Deliverables
1) After reload, update interactive state:
  - skills/prompts/themes collections
  - slash/template/skill command registry
  - any header “loaded resources” display

2) Rebuild autocomplete sources (depends on autocomplete workstream).

3) Surface diagnostics:
  - models.json error (from `ModelRegistry::error()`)
  - resource loader diagnostics

4) Extensions reload integration is explicitly **blocked** by ongoing extensions work; do not step on that surface.

# Testing
- State tests for `/reload` showing correct counts and applying new resources.
- Unit tests for diagnostic formatting.

# Dependencies
- Autocomplete workstream (`bd-1iwi`) for rebuilding autocomplete.

## Acceptance Criteria
[ ] /reload updates resources in running session
[ ] Autocomplete sources rebuild after reload
[ ] models.json and resource diagnostics surfaced
